subject: Chemistry
q:
Assume carbon burns according to following equation :
2C(s)+O2( g)→2CO (s) 
when 12 g carbon is burnt in 48 g of oxygen, the volume of carbon monoxide produced is ______×10-1 L at STP [nearest integer]

[Given : Assume CO as ideal gas, Mass of C is 12 g mol-1, mass of O is 16 g mol-1 and molar volume of an idal gas at STP is 22.7 L mol-1 ]
question:
Assume carbon burns according to following equation :
2C(s)+O2( g)→2CO (s) 
when 12 g carbon is burnt in 48 g of oxygen, the volume of carbon monoxide produced is ______×10-1 L at STP [nearest integer]

[Given : Assume CO as ideal gas, Mass of C is 12 g mol-1, mass of O is 16 g mol-1 and molar volume of an idal gas at STP is 22.7 L mol-1 ]
solution:
Combustion of carbon under less supply of air,
2C+O2→2CO    ....(X)

1212 4832                (Given moles of reactants)
Thus, carbon is the limiting reagent as for 1.5 mol of Oxygen, 3 moles of carbon is required.

Thus, from equation(X) we get,

Mole of CO=1 mole

From arrhenius law,
VCOatSTP=22.7Lit

=227×10-1Lit

subject: Chemistry
q:
The minimum amount of O2g consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction:

(Given atomic mass: Fe=56, O=16, Mg=24, P=31, C=12,H=1 )
question:
The minimum amount of O2g consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction:

(Given atomic mass: Fe=56, O=16, Mg=24, P=31, C=12,H=1 )
options: <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
(i) 5 mole O2 required = 1 mole of P4 =124 gm P4

for per gram, O2  required = 5124 mole 

(ii) 1 mole O2 required = 2 mole Mg = 2×24 gm

for per gram, O2  required =148 mole

(iii) 3 mole O2 required = 2 mole Fe = 4×56 gm

for per gram Fe, O2 required =3112×2 mole

(iv) 5 mole O2 required - =1 mole C3H8 =44 gm C3H8

per gram C3H8, O2 required =544 mole

subject: Chemistry
q:
The mole fraction of a solute in a 100 molal aqueous solution ________ ×10-2.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Given : Atomic masses: H :1.0 u, O :16.0 u]
question:
The mole fraction of a solute in a 100 molal aqueous solution ________ ×10-2.

(Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Given : Atomic masses: H :1.0 u, O :16.0 u]
solution:
100 molal aqueous solution means there is 100 mole solute in 1 kg=1000 gm water.

Now,

mole-fraction of solute=nsolute nsolute +nsolvent 

=100100+100018=18002800=0.6428

=64.28×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q: Density of 3 M NaCl solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molality of the solution is :
question: Density of 3 M NaCl solution is $1.25 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$. The molality of the solution is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 3 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaCl}, \mathrm{d}_{\text {sol }}=1.25 \mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{mol} \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { Molality } & =\frac{\mathrm{M} \times 1000}{1000 \mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{W}}} \\ & =\frac{3000}{1250-175.5}=2.79\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

options: <math><mn>24</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon and <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>18</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon annd <math><mn>7</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>22</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of carbon and <math><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
<math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> carbon and <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of hydrogen
solution:
2 mole of CO2 contains 2 mole of carbon, Hence weight of carbon =2×12=24 g .
mass of Hydrogen =25-24=1 gram

subject: Chemistry
q:
The most abundant elements by mass in the body of a healthy human adult are:

Oxygen (61.4%); Carbon (22.9%), Hydrogen (10.0%); and Nitrogen (2.6%). The weight which a 75kg person would gain if all  1H atoms are replaced by  2H atoms is:
question:
The most abundant elements by mass in the body of a healthy human adult are:

Oxygen (61.4%); Carbon (22.9%), Hydrogen (10.0%); and Nitrogen (2.6%). The weight which a 75kg person would gain if all  1H atoms are replaced by  2H atoms is:
solution:
Weight of Hydrogen in body of 75 kg person =75×10100=7.5kg

On replacing  1H by  2H , the new weight of H=7.5×2=15kg

Weight increase = 7.5kg

subject: Chemistry
q: Using the rules for significant figures, the correct answer for the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02858</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>112</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5702</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> will be:
question: Using the rules for significant figures, the correct answer for the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02858</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>112</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5702</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> will be:
solution:
In multiplication and division, the result must be reported with no more significant figures as in the measurement with few significant figures

0.02858×0.1120.5702=0.000561

(Or) Reported answer should not be more precise than the least precise term in calculations, so there should be three significant figures in reported answer.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following have same number of significant figures?
(A) 0.00253
(B) 1.0003
(C) 15.0
(D)163
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Which of the following have same number of significant figures?
(A) 0.00253
(B) 1.0003
(C) 15.0
(D)163
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
All nonzero digits are significant. All zeros that are found between nonzero digits are significant. Leading zeros (to the left of the first nonzero digit) are not significant. Trailing zeros for a whole number that ends with a decimal point are significant.The number of significant figures of the given numbers are as follows:
Numbers                   No. of significant figures
(A) 0.00253                 3  
(B) 1.0003                   5
(C) 15.0                        3
(D) 163                         3
∴ (A), (C) and (D) have the same number of significant figures.

(Rounded off to the nearest integer) [Given : Atomic weight in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>Na</mi><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>23</mn><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>14</mn><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>16</mn></math>]
question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NaNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> weighed out to make <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of an aqueous solution containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mL</mi></math> is_ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math>. (Rounded off to the nearest integer) [Given : Atomic weight in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>Na</mi><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>23</mn><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>14</mn><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>16</mn></math>]
solution:
Na+ present in 50 ml

=70 mg1 ml×50 ml=3500 mg=3.5 gm

moles of Na+=3.523= moles of NaNO3

weight of NaNO3=3.523×85=12.993 gm =13 gm

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of gaseous hydrocarbon on combustion gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of water vapour. Total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in the hydrocarbon is _______.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of gaseous hydrocarbon on combustion gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of water vapour. Total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in the hydrocarbon is _______.
solution:
The combustion reaction can be represented as 

CxHy+x+y4O2→xCO2+yH2O

At STP, 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 L

10 mL of hydrocarbon, 50 mL of water and 40 mL of carbon dioxide corresponds to 0.45, 2.23,  and 1.79 moles respectively.

Hence, the combustion of 1 mole of hydrocarbon will produce 5 moles of water and 4 moles of carbon dioxide.

Thus, the molecular formula contains 4 carbon atoms and 10 hydrogen atoms.

The hydrocarbon is C4H10 and the combustion reaction is 

C4H10+6.5O2→4CO2+5H2O

x+y=4+10=14

Hence the Total number of carbon and hydrogen atoms in the hydrocarbon is 14.

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating $2.76$ g of silver carbonate?
(Given: Molar mass of C, O and Ag are $12$, $16$ and $108$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
question:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating $2.76$ g of silver carbonate?
(Given: Molar mass of C, O and Ag are $12$, $16$ and $108$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
solution:
Molar mass of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $2 \times 108 + 12 + 3 \times 16 = 276$ g mol$^{-1}$

Number of moles of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $\dfrac{2.76}{276} = 0.01$ mol

The decomposition reaction of silver carbonate on heating is:

$Ag_2CO_3(s) \rightarrow 2Ag(s) + CO_2(g) + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2(g)$

From the stoichiometry of the reaction, $1$ mole of $Ag_2CO_3$ gives $2$ moles of Ag as residue.

Number of moles of Ag formed = $2 \times 0.01 = 0.02$ mol

Mass of Ag residue = $0.02 \times 108 = 2.16$ g

Answer: $2.16$ g

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of moles of methane required to produce \(11 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})\) after complete combustion is :
(Given molar mass of methane in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: 16\))
question:
The number of moles of methane required to produce \(11 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})\) after complete combustion is :
(Given molar mass of methane in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}: 16\))
solution:
$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{H}_{2 \mathrm{n}+2}+\frac{3 \mathrm{n}+1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{nCO}_2+(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

$0.25 \mathrm{~mol~} \mathrm{CH}_4$ gives $0.25 \mathrm{~mole}$ (or $11 \mathrm{gm}$ ) $\mathrm{CO}_2$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Butane reacts with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water following the equation given below
$\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
If 174.0 kg of butane is mixed with 320.0 kg of $\mathrm{O}_2$, the volume of water formed in litres is ________.(Nearest integer)
[Given : (a) Molar mass of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}$ are 12, 1, $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, (b) Density of water $\left.=1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\right]$
question:
Butane reacts with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water following the equation given below
$\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
If 174.0 kg of butane is mixed with 320.0 kg of $\mathrm{O}_2$, the volume of water formed in litres is ________.(Nearest integer)
[Given : (a) Molar mass of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}$ are 12, 1, $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, (b) Density of water $\left.=1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\right]$
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_2+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& 3 \times 10^3 \quad 10 \times 10^3
\end{aligned}$
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ formed $=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=5 \times \frac{2}{13} \times 10 \times 10^3$
Then $\mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=\frac{10^5}{13} \times 18$
$=1.3846 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~g}$
Volume of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ will be $=138.46$ litre.
Ans. 138

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I
Mass of substance		List-II
Number of atoms
A. $1.8$ mg waterI. $2\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
B. $9.8$ mg sulphuric acidII. $1.5\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
C. $1.8$ mg carbonIII. $3\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
D. $5.85$ mg salt (NaCl)IV. $7\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-I
Mass of substance		List-II
Number of atoms
A. $1.8$ mg waterI. $2\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
B. $9.8$ mg sulphuric acidII. $1.5\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
C. $1.8$ mg carbonIII. $3\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
D. $5.85$ mg salt (NaCl)IV. $7\times 10^{-4}\times N_A$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
For A: Molar mass of $H_2O = 18$ g/mol. Moles of $H_2O = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{18} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $H_2O$ molecule has $3$ atoms, the number of atoms = $3 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For B: Molar mass of $H_2SO_4 = 98$ g/mol. Moles of $H_2SO_4 = \dfrac{9.8 \times 10^{-3}}{98} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $H_2SO_4$ molecule has $7$ atoms, the number of atoms = $7 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For C: Molar mass of $C = 12$ g/mol. Moles of $C = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$. The number of atoms = $1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

For D: Molar mass of $NaCl = 58.5$ g/mol. Moles of $NaCl = \dfrac{5.85 \times 10^{-3}}{58.5} = 10^{-4}$. Since each $NaCl$ formula unit has $2$ atoms, the number of atoms = $2 \times 10^{-4} \times N_A$.

Thus, the correct matching is A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I

subject: Chemistry
q:
In the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
question:
In the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
options: $11.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of HCl consumed.
$33.6 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is produced regardless of temperature and pressure for every mole of Al that reacts.
$67.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of Al that reacts.
solution:
Reaction: $2\text{Al}(s) + 6\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{Al}^{3+}(aq) + 6\text{Cl}^-(aq) + 3\text{H}_2(g)$
Checking each option:
(1) Per 1 mole HCl: From stoichiometry, 6 mol HCl produces 3 mol H₂, so 1 mol HCl produces 0.5 mol H₂ = 11.2 L at STP. TRUE
(2) Per 1 mole Al: 2 mol Al produces 3 mol H₂, so 1 mol Al produces 1.5 mol H₂ = 33.6 L at STP only, not regardless of T and P. FALSE
(3) Volume ratio at same T,P: 6 mol HCl : 3 mol H₂ = 2:1, so 12 L HCl : 6 L H₂. TRUE
(4) Per 1 mole Al: 1.5 mol H₂ = 33.6 L, not 67.2 L. FALSE
Option (1) is correct.

q: The ammonia <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> released on quantitative reaction of <math><mn>0.6 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> urea <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> with sodium hydroxide <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> can be neutralized by
question: The ammonia <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> released on quantitative reaction of <math><mn>0.6 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> urea <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> with sodium hydroxide <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> can be neutralized by
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>
solution:
NH2CONH2 + 2NaOH →2NH3 +Na2CO3

By, stoichiometry, 1 mol of urea gives 2 mol NH3

Moles of urea ==0.6 gm60 gm/mol=0.01 mol

So, 0.01 mole urea produce 0.02 mole NH3

To neutralize ammonia HCl is required

NH3 +HCl→ NH4Cl

By equation, it is clear that 1 mol of ammonia is neutralized by 1mol of HCl.

So, 0.02 mol of ammonia neutralized by 0.02 mol of HCl

∴Mole of HCl=0.02 mole

Equivalent of HCl=normality×volume=0.2 N×100 mL=0.02 equivalent

As n-factor of HCl is 1, so number of equivalents=number of moles=0.02 moles

Hence, option 3 is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: When $\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is passed over red hot coke it partially gets reduced to $\mathrm{CO}(g)$. Upon passing $0.5 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2(g)$ over red hot coke, the total volume of the gases increased to $700 \mathrm{~mL}$. The composition of the gaseous mixture at STP is
question: When $\mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is passed over red hot coke it partially gets reduced to $\mathrm{CO}(g)$. Upon passing $0.5 \mathrm{~L}$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2(g)$ over red hot coke, the total volume of the gases increased to $700 \mathrm{~mL}$. The composition of the gaseous mixture at STP is
solution:
$\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}$
Stoichoimetry ratio is $1: 2$
AT STP, $P=1 \mathrm{~atm}, T=273 \mathrm{~K}, R=0.0821$
Initial moles of $\mathrm{CO}_2 ; n\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right.$ initial $)=\frac{P V}{R T}$ $=\frac{1 \times 0.5}{0.0821 \times 273}=0.022 \mathrm{~mole}$
In final mixture no. of moles; $n\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}\right.$ mixture)
$$
=\frac{1 \times 0.7}{0.0821 \times 273}=0.031
$$
Increase in volume is by $=0.031-0.022$ $=0.009$ mole of gas
Final no. of moles of $\mathrm{CO}$ i.e. $n_{\text {(CO final) }}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=2 n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 \text { initial }\right)}-n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right. \text { final) }} \\
& \quad=2\left(0.022-\mathrm{n}_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2 \text { final }\right)}\right. \\
& \left.n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-2 n_{\text {(CO }} \text { final }\right) \\
& \therefore \text { Now, } n_{\text {(CO final) }}+n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.031 \\
& n_{\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right. \text { final) }}=0.031-n_{\text {(CO final) })} \ldots \text { (iii) }
\end{aligned}
$$
Substituting (ii) in eq. (i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-2\left[0.031-n_{\text {(CO final) }}\right. \\
& n_{\text {(CO final) }}=0.044-0.062+2 n_{\text {(CO final })}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
n_{(\mathrm{CO} \text { final) }}=0.018 \mathrm{~mol} .
$$
Volume of $\mathrm{CO}=V=\frac{n R T}{P}=\frac{0.018 \times 0.0821 \times 273}{1}$ $=0.40$ Litre
and volume of $\mathrm{CO}_2=0.7$ litre $-0.4$ litre $=0.3$ litre
$$
\therefore \mathrm{CO}_2=300 \mathrm{~mL}, \mathrm{CO}=400 \mathrm{~mL}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
question: The hydrocarbon $(X)$ with molar mass $80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $90 \%$ carbon has $\ldots\ldots$ degree of unsaturation.
solution: $\therefore$ Empirical formula is $\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4$ Molecular mass $=80 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Molecular formula $=\left(\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_4\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$ $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\text { Molecular mass }}{\mathrm{EF} \text { mass }}=\frac{80}{40}=2$ $\therefore$ Molecular formula is $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_8$ Degree of unsaturation $=\frac{2 \times 6+2-8}{2}=3$

question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an organic compound which contains only carbon and hydrogen on complete combustion gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>330</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>270</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of water. The percentage of carbon and hydrogen in the organic compound are respectively
solution:
Weight of the organic compound, W=120g,

Weight of carbondioxide (x)=330g

Weight of water (y)=270g

Percentage of carbon=1244XxWX100                                       =1244X330120X100                                        =75%Percentage of Hydrogen =218XyWX100                                       =218X270120X100                                        =25%

Number of mole of sodium =823

=9.00

q:
Match List-I with List-II
  List-I   List-II
A 16 g of CH4 (g) I Weighs 28g
B 1 g of H2(g) II 60.2 × 1023 electrons
C 1 mole of N2 (g) III Weighs 32g
D 0.5 mol of SO2(g) IV Occupies11.4 L volume at STP

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List-I with List-II
  List-I   List-II
A 16 g of CH4 (g) I Weighs 28g
B 1 g of H2(g) II 60.2 × 1023 electrons
C 1 mole of N2 (g) III Weighs 32g
D 0.5 mol of SO2(g) IV Occupies11.4 L volume at STP

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The number of moles =MassMolar mass

The number of molecules = moles×NA

One mole of any gas occupies 22.7 L at STP.

(A) Moles of CH4=1616=1
Molecules =6.02×1023
Electrons =10×6.02×1023
=60.2×1023

(B) Moles of H2=12=0.5 mole
Volume at STP =12×22.7=11.35 lit .

(C) 1 mole N2=28gm

(D) 0.5 mole SO2=12×64=32gm

q:
Xg of benzoic acid on reaction with aq $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ released $\mathrm{CO}_2$ that occupied 11.2 L volume at STP.
X is _____ g.
question:
Xg of benzoic acid on reaction with aq $\mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ released $\mathrm{CO}_2$ that occupied 11.2 L volume at STP.
X is _____ g.
solution: 1 mole benzoic acid will release 1 mole $\mathrm{CO}_2$ 11.2 $\mathrm{L} \mathrm{CO}_2$ at STP is $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mole}_{\mathrm{CO}}^2$ which will be released by reaction with $\frac{1}{2}$ mole benzoic acid $\begin{aligned} \text { Mass of benzoic acid } & =\frac{1}{2} \times 122 \\ & =61 \mathrm{gm}\end{aligned}$

q: A $5.2$ molal aqueous solution of methyl alcohol, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$, is supplied. What is the mole fraction of methyl alcohol in the solution?
question: A $5.2$ molal aqueous solution of methyl alcohol, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$, is supplied. What is the mole fraction of methyl alcohol in the solution?
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Molefraction of solute }\left(\mathrm{X}_2\right) \text { in aqueous solution } & =\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{m}+\frac{1000}{18}} \\ & =\frac{5.2}{5.2+\frac{1000}{18}}=0.09\end{aligned}$

q: How many moles of magnesium phosphate, $\mathrm{Mg}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$ will contain $0.25$ mole of oxygen atoms?
question: How many moles of magnesium phosphate, $\mathrm{Mg}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$ will contain $0.25$ mole of oxygen atoms?
solution:
$\mathrm{Mg}_3\left(\mathrm{PO}_4\right)_2$
'n' moles
$8 \mathrm{n}=0.25$
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{0.25}{8}$
$\quad=\frac{25}{8 \times 100}=3.125 \times 10^{-2}$

q: The formula of a gaseous hydrocarbon which requires 6 times of its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for complete oxidation and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is ________.
question: The formula of a gaseous hydrocarbon which requires 6 times of its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for complete oxidation and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is ________.
solution:
Combustion reaction.

CxHy(g)+x+y4O2g→xCO2g+y2H2Oℓ

V               6 V             --                -                        Vx=4V

⇒x=4

Since: I Vo2=6×VCxHy

⇒Vx+y4=6 V

⇒x+y4=6⇒4+y4=6

⇒y=8

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
question: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
solution:
Let the mass of oxygen be x gm
∴   Mass of nitrogen = 4x gm
Number of molecules = 6.023 x 1023 x number of moles
∴   Ratio = 6.023 × 1 0 2 3 × no. of moles of oxygen 6.023 × 1 0 2 3 × no. of moles of nitrogen
              = m O 2 M O 2 m N 2 M N 2 = x 3 2 4 x 2 8
              = 7 3 2 = 7 : 32
